name: Qdrant
what_it_does: Qdrant is a high-performance open-source vector database and search engine written in Rust, designed specifically for AI and machine learning workloads. It stores high-dimensional embedding vectors alongside arbitrary JSON payloads and enables fast approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search with rich metadata filtering. Qdrant supports dense vectors, sparse vectors (BM42), and multi-vector configurations — enabling hybrid search that combines semantic similarity with keyword relevance in a single query. Available as self-hosted (Docker/Kubernetes) or fully managed via Qdrant Cloud, with an official MCP server for direct AI agent memory integration.
use_cases: RAG pipelines with semantic document retrieval and rich metadata filtering (date ranges, categories, user IDs)
Hybrid search combining dense embedding vectors with sparse BM42 keyword vectors in a single query
AI agent persistent semantic memory via the official MCP server (qdrant-store / qdrant-find tools)
Recommendation systems using vector similarity plus business logic payload filters
Real-time vector search at scale with on-disk HNSW indexing for datasets exceeding RAM
Multi-tenant vector databases using collection-level isolation or payload-based filtering with named vectors
not_for: Teams that want relational data with complex multi-table joins (use PostgreSQL instead)
Pure keyword search without semantic understanding (use Elasticsearch or Typesense)
Workloads requiring ACID transactions across multiple documents
Teams without any DevOps capability who need a fully managed zero-touch database
best_when: You need a high-performance, resource-efficient vector database with first-class MCP server support, the ability to self-host for free, or a competitively priced managed cloud option — especially for RAG, semantic search, or agent memory.
avoid_when: Your workload is purely keyword-based full-text search, or you need relational database guarantees like foreign keys and complex joins.

alternatives.reason: Fully managed with no self-hosting option; simpler but pricier at scale
Built-in vectorizer modules and GraphQL API; more opinionated pipeline
The MCP-only interface for Qdrant without direct REST/gRPC access

auth.notes: API key auth via X-API-Key header or Bearer token in Authorization header. Qdrant Cloud provides project-scoped API keys; read-only and full-access key types supported. Self-hosted instances have no auth by default — must be explicitly configured. No RBAC or fine-grained scopes beyond read vs write.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free cluster on Qdrant Cloud: 1GB RAM, 0.5 vCPU, 4GB disk — self-hosted is completely unlimited
pricing.paid_tiers: Qdrant Cloud from ~$0.014/hour per vCPU-GB RAM; Enterprise plan with custom SLAs available
pricing.notes: Apache-2.0 licensed open source. Self-hosting costs only infrastructure. Qdrant Cloud free tier is permanent (not a trial). Among the most cost-efficient managed vector databases at scale.

performance.rate_limits: No hard rate limits on self-hosted; Qdrant Cloud scales limits with plan size

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API returns structured JSON errors with status code, error type, and detailed description. gRPC uses standard gRPC status codes. Collection not found, vector size mismatch, and payload validation errors all produce clear, actionable messages. MCP server errors bubble up from the underlying REST client.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Points (vectors) identified by UUID or unsigned int. Upsert operations are fully idempotent — inserting the same point ID replaces the existing point rather than duplicating. Batch upserts are safe to retry. Collection creation with same name/config is idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Collection must be created with the correct vector dimensionality before insertion — vector size mismatches produce hard errors, not coercions
gRPC is significantly faster than REST for bulk upserts — use gRPC or SDK batch methods for ingestion pipelines, not repeated single-point REST calls
Payload indexes must be explicitly created (create_payload_index) for filtered search performance — without indexes, filtered search degrades to O(n) full collection scan
Sparse vector search (BM42) requires explicit sparse_vectors config at collection creation — cannot be added to existing dense-only collections
MCP server only exposes two tools (qdrant-store, qdrant-find) — not a full database API; direct REST/gRPC needed for collection management, deletion, or upserts with custom IDs
MCP server uses FastEmbed for embedding — first startup downloads the default model (~40MB); EMBEDDING_MODEL env var controls which model is used
QDRANT_URL and QDRANT_LOCAL_PATH are mutually exclusive in the MCP server — setting both causes startup failure
Quantization (scalar, product, binary) must be configured at collection creation — cannot apply to existing data without recreating the collection
